name: Fiji (Nadi and the Mamanuca and Yasawa Islands)
teaser: Fiji is a Melanesian archipelago of more than 330 islands in the South Pacific. Nadi (pronounced Nandi) on the western coast of Viti Levu is the country's tourism gateway. From here the Mamanuca and Yasawa islands (resort and backpacker islands respectively) and the Coral Coast spread out across some of the world's clearest tropical water.
intro: Fiji is a Melanesian archipelago of more than 330 islands in the South Pacific. Nadi (pronounced Nandi) on the western coast of Viti Levu is the country's tourism gateway. From here the Mamanuca and Yasawa islands (resort and backpacker islands respectively) and the Coral Coast spread out across some of the world's clearest tropical water.
whyVisit:
Fiji is one of the planet's signature South Pacific destinations and the cultural heart of Melanesia. Coral reefs, soft-coral diving in the Bligh Water, and Fijian bula hospitality (an actual greeting and an actual cultural value) define the experience.

The country is also Indigenous-led tourism done relatively well. Most islands are owned by traditional village communities (mataqali), which have a real say in how resorts and tour operators work on their vanua (land and sea). Visiting respectfully means engaging the village protocol that underpins everything.

ethicalCompass.economy: Choose Fijian-owned resorts and village stays where possible; locally owned operators in the Mamanucas, Yasawas, and Coral Coast keep more money in Fijian communities than international chains. Eat at kana (Fijian restaurants) and Indo-Fijian curry houses in Nadi town, not just the resort buffets.
ethicalCompass.employment: Tipping is not customary in Fiji; staff tip pools at resorts are appreciated but small. Buy from women's village handicraft groups directly rather than airport gift shops where margins are taken upstream.
ethicalCompass.education: Learn the sevusevu protocol: when visiting any traditional village, present yaqona (kava root) to the turaga ni koro (village headman) through an introduction. Most tour operators handle this; do not skip it. Wear sulu (sarong) covering knees in villages; remove hats in chiefly presence.
ethicalCompass.ecology: Fiji's reefs are under pressure from bleaching and overfishing. Choose dive operators who follow the Mamanuca Environment Society or comparable certification. Reef-safe sunscreen only. Do not stand on coral. Avoid resorts on artificially nourished beaches if alternatives exist.

gm: Many travellers are visa-exempt for four months in Fiji; verify on the official Fijian Department of Immigration portal.
Yellow fever vaccination is required if arriving from a country with risk; verify current rules.
Inter-island transfers: the Awesome Adventures Fiji "Yasawa Flyer" is the main backpacker-island ferry; South Sea Cruises runs the Mamanuca catamaran. Verify schedules on the official portals.
For village visits, always go through your accommodation or a registered operator; do not improvise a village arrival.
Dive shops: verify PADI or SSI certification and ask about their reef-protection practices.
icu: The Fiji Times and Fiji Sun for current local news (cyclone warnings matter).
Tourism Fiji (the official tourism site) for events, cyclone advisories, and operator lists.
A Fijian or Pacific author: Epeli Hau'ofa (Tongan, but Pacific-Islands central); Sia Figiel (Samoan); Vilsoni Hereniko (Rotuman-Fijian).
A Fijian-owned village-stay operator (such as Tribewanted at Vorovoro historically, or current similar projects).
Wikivoyage Fiji for island orientation and inter-island transport.
iinter: You decide on resort vs island. A Coral Coast resort is comfortable and easy; a Yasawa island-hopping trip with a Bula Pass is rougher and more authentic; a true village stay is rarer and the most meaningful.
You decide on sevusevu commitment. If you visit any traditional village, the protocol is not optional. Engage it properly or do not visit.
You decide on reef ethics. Reef-safe sunscreen, no coral touching, choose certified dive operators. The reefs are visibly degraded; your behaviour matters at the margin.

You decide on Indo-Fijian engagement. Roughly a third of Fiji is Indo-Fijian (descendants of Indian indentured workers brought during British rule). The food (Suva and Nadi curry houses, kakana ni jambu), temples, and music are part of the country.
You decide on cyclone risk. Tropical cyclones can hit November to April; build flexibility into bookings.
gyroscopePrep.ico:
Fiji weather is tropical and dramatic. Wet season (November to April) brings cyclones; dry season (May to October) is the practical visitor window. Inter-island ferries are weather-sensitive; flights from Nadi to the islands are vulnerable to wind.

Bring a soft plan. If a cyclone warning closes islands, Nadi-based excursions (Sleeping Giant Garden, the Sabeto hot springs, Suva day) still work. If a dive is cancelled by swell, the Coral Coast lagoon is usually calmer. If a village visit is rerouted, your tour operator handles the local re-arrangement.

anchorSpots: A Mamanuca or Yasawa island day or overnight. Mamanucas are closer and more developed; Yasawas are more remote and offer the Blue Lagoon experience. Pick one and do it properly.
Sigatoka Sand Dunes National Park (Coral Coast). A genuine Fijian landscape and a place of cultural significance; archaeological tours possible.
A traditional village visit with sevusevu protocol. Through a registered operator, with proper introduction. Worth doing once at minimum.
Diving the Mamanuca soft corals or the Beqa Lagoon shark dive. Beqa is the famous bull-shark dive; the Mamanuca reefs are gentler and beautiful.
A day in Nadi town, the Sri Siva Subramaniya Temple, and the Sabeto Valley. Indo-Fijian temple, hot springs, mud-bath, and a Fijian curry lunch.
recommendedLength: Five to ten days at minimum to justify the flight time for most visitors. A week lets you combine Coral Coast nights and an island stay; two weeks adds proper diving or Suva and the eastern islands.
transportHint: Fly into Nadi International Airport (NAN). On Viti Levu, buses run frequently along the Coral Coast; Pacific Transport is the main long-distance operator. To islands: South Sea Cruises to the Mamanucas, Awesome Adventures Fiji to the Yasawas, charter and seaplane for further. Rental cars work on Viti Levu but driving is left-hand and at Fijian pace.
prompt:
Please help me plan an ethical visit to Fiji (Nadi and the islands) for [NUMBER] days in [MONTH]. I am travelling with [WHO] and my interests are [INTERESTS, e.g. diving, snorkelling, village culture, beach relaxation, Indo-Fijian food]. My total budget is around [AMOUNT] and my comfort level is [budget / mid-range / higher-comfort].

Please structure your answer in three steps.

**Step 1: Choices.** Help me decide what to prioritise. Recommend the two or three Fiji experiences I should not miss given my interests, and one I should consider skipping (a budget island-hopping pass that exhausts me, a "village visit" without proper sevusevu, a chain-resort buffet that crowds out a Nadi curry house). Briefly explain each trade-off.

**Step 2: Preparation.** Cover all four of the following:
- **Governance Management.** What assumptions should I check before I book? Include the Fijian Department of Immigration visa portal, official inter-island ferry operators, registered dive shops with conservation certification, and village-visit protocol through registered operators.
- **Information Curation.** Suggest at least four different source types: one official Fijian source, one Fiji news outlet for cyclone awareness, one Pacific Islands author, and one Fijian-owned operator (village stay, dive shop, or guide).
- **Inference Interaction.** List the decisions I personally need to make (resort vs island vs village stay, sevusevu commitment, reef ethics, Indo-Fijian engagement, cyclone-season risk).
- **Intelligence Cooperation.** How should I trust my own judgment and local advice over algorithmic defaults when conditions change? Build me a soft plan with at least two alternates for likely disruptions (cyclone warning, ferry cancellation by sea conditions, dive day cancelled by swell, a closed village).

**Step 3: Delivery.** Give me the actual itinerary, day by day, with realistic timings and named islands or sites. Include at least one proper village experience and one reef day. Mark each business as confidently Fijian-owned or locally owned, or flag it for me to verify.

Finally, please remind me at the end to verify your suggestions against:
1. Official sources: Tourism Fiji, the Fiji Meteorological Service for cyclone status, and the official ferry operators.
2. Real people: a Fijian guide, a village host, or resort staff who live in Fiji now.

Treat your output as a researcher's draft. I will make the final calls.
